file_name: Marsden et al. - 2023 - BT03 (P63) Effectiveness of an image-analysing artificial intelligence-based digital health technolo.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper (BT03) describes the DERM-003 study, which evaluated an artificial intelligence-based digital health technology (AI-DHT) to diagnose nonmelanoma skin cancer (SCC, BCC) and premalignant lesions in dermoscopic images. The AI-DHT output was compared with histopathology results, and diagnostic performance metrics (AUROC, sensitivity, specificity, PPV, NPV) were calculated.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The text identifies the work as 'BT03 (P63)', a conference abstract presenting original results from the 'DERM-003 study', which is described as a 'prospective multi centre single-arm masked study'. The abstract reports primary data collection involving 572 patients, diagnostic performance metrics (AUROC, sensitivity, specificity) of an AI-based digital health technology, and histopathology comparisons, which constitutes original experimental research rather than a review of prior literature.

title: Effectiveness of an image-analysing artificial intelligence-based digital health technology to diagnose nonmelanoma skin cancer and benign skin lesions
brief_summary: The DERM-003 study evaluated the effectiveness of an artificial intelligence-based digital health technology (AI-DHT) for diagnosing nonmelanoma skin cancer and premalignant lesions in dermoscopic images. This prospective, multi-center, single-arm masked study recruited 572 patients from four UK NHS trusts, capturing images of suspicious skin lesions using three smartphone cameras. The AI-DHT's diagnostic output was compared against histopathology results, which served as the reference standard. The primary evaluation metric was the area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUROC) for detecting squamous cell carcinoma (SCC) and basal cell carcinoma (BCC). The AI-DHT demonstrated high diagnostic performance, with AUROCs ranging from 0.85 to 0.89 for SCC and BCC across different devices. The study concluded that the AI-DHT has the potential to support the diagnosis of nonmelanoma skin cancer and premalignant lesions.

lesion_types_studied.details: Primary target for diagnostic performance evaluation (AUROC, sensitivity, specificity, PPV, NPV) in the DERM-003 study; 47 biopsied lesions diagnosed as SCC.
Primary target for diagnostic performance evaluation (AUROC, sensitivity, specificity, PPV, NPV) in the DERM-003 study; 184 biopsied lesions diagnosed as BCC.
Secondary endpoint of the DERM-003 study; included specific subtypes such as Bowen disease, actinic keratosis, and dysplastic lesions.
12 lesions diagnosed as Bowen disease were all classified correctly by the AI-DHT.
52 of 61 lesions diagnosed as actinic keratosis were correctly classified by the AI-DHT.
18 of 22 lesions diagnosed as dysplastic were correctly classified by the AI-DHT.

All 16 lesions diagnosed as melanoma were classified as such by the AI-DHT; mentioned in title as 'nonmelanoma skin cancer' implying melanoma was part of the broader diagnostic context or exclusion/verification set.
Included in the study title; the AI-DHT was evaluated for diagnosing nonmelanoma skin cancer and benign skin lesions.
Mentioned in the context of clinical diagnosis by dermatology consultants for lesions referred for surgery, but not explicitly detailed as a primary AI-DHT evaluation target in the DERM-003 results.

all_task_types.target: Diagnostic categories of skin lesions (Squamous cell carcinoma, Basal cell carcinoma, premalignant conditions, melanoma, benign lesions)
all_task_types.details: The AI-DHT was used to diagnose nonmelanoma skin cancer and benign skin lesions. The study evaluated diagnostic performance using AUROC, sensitivity, specificity, PPV, and NPV for detecting SCC and BCC, and assessed the correct classification of premalignant lesions (Bowen disease, actinic keratosis, dysplastic lesions) and melanoma against histopathology reference standards.

datasets_used.name: DERM-003 study cohort
datasets_used.description: A prospective multi-centre single-arm masked study cohort recruited from four UK National Health Service (NHS) trusts. It consisted of patients with at least one suspicious skin lesion suitable for photography, captured using three smartphone cameras (iPhone 6S, iPhone 11, and Samsung 10) with a dermoscopic lens attached.
classes_used: Premalignant conditions (including Bowen disease, actinic keratosis, dysplastic lesions)

use.testing: The dataset was used to evaluate the diagnostic performance (AUROC, sensitivity, specificity, PPV, NPV) of the AI-based digital health technology (AI-DHT) against histopathology results as the reference standard.
use.other: The study also assessed the AI-DHT's ability to correctly classify premalignant lesions and melanoma.

statistical_methods_used.method: Area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUROC)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the diagnostic performance of the AI-DHT for detecting squamous cell carcinoma (SCC) and basal cell carcinoma (BCC).
To provide uncertainty estimation for AUROC, sensitivity, specificity, positive predictive value (PPV), and negative predictive value (NPV) metrics.
To assess the ability of the AI-DHT to correctly identify SCC and BCC in biopsied lesions.
To assess the ability of the AI-DHT to correctly identify non-cancerous lesions.
To assess the probability that a positive AI-DHT diagnosis corresponds to a histopathology-confirmed diagnosis.
To assess the probability that a negative AI-DHT diagnosis corresponds to a histopathology-confirmed non-cancerous diagnosis.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values or significance tests). Diagnostic performance was evaluated using AUROC, sensitivity, specificity, PPV, NPV, and their 95% confidence intervals.

DERM-003 study cohort.metric: AUROC, Sensitivity, Specificity, Positive Predictive Value (PPV), Negative Predictive Value (NPV), Correct Classification counts
DERM-003 study cohort.notes: For premalignant lesions and melanoma, the AI-DHT correctly classified all 12 Bowen disease lesions, 52 of 61 actinic keratosis lesions, 18 of 22 dysplastic lesions, and all 16 melanoma lesions. The study included 572 patients (49.5% women, mean age 68.5 years, 96.9% Fitzpatrick skin types I–III) recruited from four UK NHS trusts. 395 lesions had histopathology results (47 SCC, 184 BCC).

architecture_details.description: The paper describes the use of an AI-DHT to assess dermoscopic images but does not report the specific underlying AI architecture, model family, backbone, or technical implementation details.

conclusions: The artificial intelligence-based digital health technology (AI-DHT) demonstrated high diagnostic performance for detecting squamous cell carcinoma (SCC) and basal cell carcinoma (BCC) across different smartphone cameras (iPhone 6S, iPhone 11, and Samsung 10), with AUROCs ranging from 0.85 to 0.89.
The AI-DHT showed high sensitivity for detecting SCC (98%) and BCC (94%) but lower specificity (38% for SCC and 28% for BCC).

The AI-DHT correctly classified all 12 lesions diagnosed as Bowen disease, 52 of 61 lesions diagnosed as actinic keratosis, 18 of 22 lesions diagnosed as dysplastic, and all 16 lesions diagnosed as melanoma.
The study concludes that the AI-DHT has the potential to support the diagnosis of nonmelanoma skin cancer (NMSC) and premalignant lesions.
important_remarks: The study is a conference abstract (BT03/P63) rather than a full peer-reviewed paper, limiting the depth of methodological detail available.
The AI-DHT demonstrated high sensitivity for SCC (98%) and BCC (94%) but notably low specificity (38% for SCC and 28% for BCC), indicating a high false-positive rate.
The study population was heavily biased towards lighter skin types, with 96.9% of the 572 patients having Fitzpatrick skin types I–III, limiting generalizability to darker skin tones.

The AI-DHT was evaluated across three different smartphone cameras (iPhone 6S, iPhone 11, Samsung 10), showing consistent AUROC performance (0.85–0.89) across devices, which supports robustness but may not reflect real-world variability in image quality.
The study is a single-arm masked study from four UK NHS trusts; lack of external validation on independent datasets or diverse geographic populations is a limitation.
No specific AI architecture or model details were reported, only the output of the 'artificial intelligence-based digital health technology' (AI-DHT).
The study did not report formal hypothesis testing (p-values), relying instead on point estimates and 95% confidence intervals for diagnostic metrics.

Premalignant lesion classification was assessed via correct classification counts rather than standard sensitivity/specificity metrics, with variable performance (e.g., 52/61 for actinic keratosis vs. 100% for Bowen disease).
